title: feat: version control community-model monitor deploy configs
author: voodoohop
pr_body_excerpt:
## Summary
- Adds `scripts/community-monitor/` — CYCLE.md, probe.mjs, watchdog.sh (actual deploy path), loop.sh + systemd unit (intended fast-follow), README, .env.example. Previously these only lived ad-hoc on the EC2 box with no history.
- Pins the monitor agent to `--model sonnet --effort low` (was unpinned/default) — a 15-min mechanical health-check loop doesn't need frontier reasoning.
- Adds a cycle-counted self-`/compact` every 20 cycles (~5h) to CYCLE.md so the long-lived `--remote-control` session doesn't grow context forever.

Deployed live to the box already; this PR just brings the source under version control.

## Test plan
- [x] Verified `sonnet`/`low effort` pin active on the running remote-control session
- [x] Verified a full cycle completes correctly with `cyclesSinceCompact` incrementing per CYCLE.md's new duty 0
- [x] `.env`/`state.json`/`people_mapping.json`/logs correctly gitignored (secrets + PII stay off git)

gist.headline: Taming the Watchdog
gist.blurb: We've brought our community model monitor under version control. Now our health-check agent runs leaner on Sonnet and cleans up its own context history.
gist.summary: Moved the community-monitor deployment scripts into version control, pinned the agent to Sonnet with low effort, and added a self-compaction cycle every 5 hours to prevent context bloat.
gist.impact: This ensures the monitoring agent runs reliably without consuming excessive context or requiring manual restarts on the EC2 instance.
gist.visual_concept: Polly the bee organizing files or putting a leash on a cute robotic watchdog near a retro server rack.
gist.image_prompt: Cozy pixel art scene of the cute bee mascot organizing glowing code files into a neat folder, while a small friendly robotic watchdog sits nearby. A retro server rack with blinking lights stands in the background. Soft lime green (#ecf874) and pastel lavender gradient background. Chunky retro sprites, warm lighting, lo-fi vibes like Stardew Valley. Nostalgic but beautiful, emotionally warm.
